---
title: "Data Ethics Case Study 12: Generative AI, Web Scraping, and Copyright"
subtitle: "Case Studies in Data Governance & Relational Systems"
author: "Brendan Shea, PhD"
date: last-modified
categories: [AI Ethics, Web Scraping, Copyright, Governance]
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    css: styles.css
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: show
    code-summary: "Show Database/SQL Reference"
    embed-resources: true
---

## Executive Summary

**Generative AI** tools are growing very fast. These tools learn from massive amounts of data. Scrapers harvest text, images, and code from the web. They do this automatically. But many creators are very angry. They say their work is taken without **consent**. They get no pay and no credit. 

This case study looks at web scraping and copyright laws. We explore the ethical borders of AI training datasets. We show how databases can track **data history** (also called **data provenance**). This history helps companies respect choices and avoid legal risks.

::: {.callout-note}
### Core Concepts Covered
*   We study how to track **data history** and data origin.
*   We explore how databases respect creator choice and opt-out requests.
*   We learn how simple data filters protect copyright before training.
:::

---

## The Scenario

### The Illusion of the Magic Machine

In the classic story *The Wizard of Oz*, visitors travel to the Emerald City. They stand before the great and powerful Wizard. The Wizard appears as a giant green head. He projects a magical illusion of power and knowledge. The citizens bow to him. They believe he is a magical force. But then, Dorothy pulls back a velvet curtain. She finds an ordinary man. He pulls mechanical levers. He speaks into a microphone. The magical Wizard is just a man using machines.

Modern **generative AI** works under a similar illusion. We type a prompt into tools like ChatGPT or Midjourney. We get an essay or a drawing in seconds. The AI feels like a magical, living brain. 

But if we pull back the curtain, we find a **database engine**. This engine runs on millions of human creations. AI models do not write or paint from their own minds. They learn by studying huge databases of human work. They find mathematical patterns in past creations.

To build these datasets, AI companies use automated **crawlers**. These programs are called **web scrapers**. **Scrapers** travel the web and harvest files.

### The History of Web Crawlers

Web crawling is not new. In the early 1990s, the public web was very small. People had to find websites using printed directories. But soon, the web grew too large. In response, computer scientists built early search engines like AltaVista, Yahoo, and Google. These companies deployed early web crawlers. The crawlers traveled the web to build a search index. This index helped users find websites easily. 

Historically, this automated harvesting was used strictly for search. Website owners were happy to be crawled. If a crawler visited their site, it meant human users could find them. This was a win-win relationship.

But today, AI companies have changed this relationship. They use crawlers to build commercial training datasets, not search indexes. They copy entire novels, creative paintings, and private software codes. They do this to build a competing product. And they often ignore whether creators want their work harvested.

The scraping process follows five basic steps:

1.  The crawler starts with a list of seed website addresses. It requests the content of those pages.

2.  The crawler reads the webpage's raw code. It strips away all formatting to isolate text and images.

3.  The program finds all links on the webpage. It adds these addresses to its target list.

4.  The program saves the text, image links, and key details (called **metadata**) into a flat database.

5.  The system repeats this process automatically. It works day and night to build a huge web index.

This recursive process is extremely aggressive. If left unchecked, a single crawler can download millions of pages in a single day. This puts a massive strain on website servers.

### The Real-World Legal and Creative Battles

This automated harvesting has caused intense real-world battles. As generative AI models grew more powerful and highly profitable, three major controversies pulled back the curtain on AI training datasets:

First, consider the **Books3 leak**. In 2021, experts found that major AI models studied a dataset called "Books3." This dataset held over 190,000 copyrighted books scraped from pirated digital libraries. Famous authors like Stephen King and Margaret Atwood found that the AI could summarize their books page-by-page. Their private books were harvested without pay or credit. The controversy was covered in depth, including by [Mashable](https://mashable.com/article/books3-ai-training-dataset-pirated-books-taken-down), which recorded the subsequent takedown of the dataset following anti-piracy DMCA requests.

Second, consider the **Getty Images lawsuit**. Visual art generators scraped millions of digital paintings and photos. In 2023, Getty Images launched a major lawsuit in London, officially announced in their [Getty Images Statement](https://newsroom.gettyimages.com/en/getty-images/getty-images-statement), alleging that Stability AI copied millions of protected photos without a license. Some AI drawings even showed warped versions of Getty's visual watermark. This proved that the AI had directly copied and memorized the copyrighted stock photos.

Third, consider open-source code wrongs. Coding tools like GitHub Copilot studied billions of lines of public code. The code was stored in online repositories. Developers noticed that the AI generated their exact algorithms, sparking a class-action lawsuit led by open-source programmers, which is documented at **[githubcopilotlitigation.com](https://githubcopilotlitigation.com/)**. Yet, the AI ignored the **open-source software licenses**. Licenses like the GPL or the MIT License require proper credit and sharing. The AI treated the code as free property, stripping away the names of the authors and ignoring the legal terms of the license.

---

## The Ethical Dilemma

This clash between automated harvesting and creative rights highlights three major ethical dilemmas.

### Copyright and Fair Use

The first conflict is about copyright law and **Fair Use**. **Fair Use** allows people to use copyrighted work without permission. Under United States law, judges look at four core factors to decide if a use is fair:

1.  The purpose and character of the use. Is it commercial or educational? Is it transformative?

2.  The nature of the copyrighted work. Is it highly creative or factual?

3.  The amount of the work used. Was a tiny snippet taken, or was the entire file copied?

4.  The effect on the market. Does the new product hurt the sales of the original creator?

The debate between AI developers and human creators can be seen in two standard arguments.

The AI developers' argument is structured as follows:

::: {.argument-block}
::: {.argument-premise}
**Premise 1**: Human artists learn to paint by studying public art in museums, which is legal.
:::
::: {.argument-premise}
**Premise 2**: AI models study public data strictly to learn abstract patterns, not to copy files directly.
:::
::: {.argument-conclusion}
**Conclusion**: Therefore, training an AI model on public data is a transformative educational act that falls under **Fair Use**.
:::
:::

AI developers argue that the final model is a completely new tool. It does not contain the original files. It only contains mathematical numbers that represent connections between words and pixels.

The content creators' argument is structured as follows:

::: {.argument-block}
::: {.argument-premise}
**Premise 1**: **Fair Use** is meant to protect human learning and education, not massive corporate harvesting.
:::
::: {.argument-premise}
**Premise 2**: AI companies build commercial tools that directly replace the creators whose work was harvested.
:::
::: {.argument-conclusion}
**Conclusion**: Therefore, scraping copyrighted work to build competing tools is a form of theft.
:::
:::

Content creators point to the fourth factor of Fair Use. They argue that if an AI can write an essay in the style of a specific author, it hurts that author's livelihood. The commercial impact is negative.

### The Illusion of Informed Consent

The second dilemma is about **consent**. When writers publish stories or code online, they expect human readers. They do not expect machines to harvest their work. They do not want companies building competing products. 

Today, websites rely on a standard called the **robots.txt** file. This text file is stored on a website's server. It tells crawlers which pages they can visit. But this old standard has three huge flaws:

1.  Compliance is entirely voluntary. Rogue scrapers can easily ignore the file. There are no database locks to stop them.

2.  Website owners face an **all-or-nothing choice**. They must allow all crawlers so humans can find them. Or they must block all crawlers and lose their web traffic.

3.  The control has no retroactive power. Once an AI developer scrapes a site, updating the text file does not remove the data from the trained model.

In the modern attention economy, consent is often an illusion. Users must click "I Agree" on long terms of service documents. They rarely read these pages. If they refuse to click, they are locked out of modern digital platforms. This is not true choice.

### Privacy and the Right to be Forgotten

The third dilemma is about personal privacy. Web scrapers act like massive digital vacuums. They harvest everything on a page. This includes **Personally Identifiable Information (PII)**. PII means personal details like email addresses, phone numbers, and sensitive forum posts.

Modern privacy laws like the **GDPR** in Europe and the **CCPA** in California protect citizens. They grant three basic rights:

*   Individuals have a **Right to Data Portability**. This lets them download a copy of their stored personal data.

*   Individuals have a **Right to Rectification**. This lets them demand that incorrect personal details be fixed.

*   Individuals have a **Right to Erasure**. This is also called the **Right to be Forgotten**. It lets them demand that all personal records be deleted.

For a standard database, deleting a user is very simple. An administrator runs a quick delete query. But once that data is used to train an AI model, a huge problem arises. 

An AI model does not store raw text files. Instead, it converts the data into millions of numbers called **weights**. These weights represent connections between ideas. Complying with the **Right to Erasure** is impossible without deleting the entire model. The company must retrain the model from scratch. This costs millions of dollars in computing power and electricity. This creates a permanent legal risk for tech companies.

---

## Database & Data Governance Considerations

To avoid lawsuits, modern tech companies must use strong **data governance** (data rules). Instead of scraping the web blindly, database engineers design schemas that enforce **data provenance** (data history). Data history is the record of where a file came from. It tracks its license and consent settings.

Database administrators use tables to store and filter crawled records before they reach the AI.

### Graphic Depiction of the Schema

The following diagram outlines an ethical tracking database. This design ensures that every scraped document is linked to its parent website, tracks crawling permissions, and logs deleted files:

```{mermaid}
erDiagram
    DOMAINS ||--o{ DOCUMENTS : "hosts"
    DOCUMENTS ||--o{ PURGE_AUDITS : "logs removal"
    
    DOMAINS {
        string domain_name
        string crawler_permission
        string creator_opt_out_status
    }
    
    DOCUMENTS {
        string website_url
        string license_type
        string compliance_approval_state
    }
    
    PURGE_AUDITS {
        string deleted_url
        string deletion_reason
        string date_purged
    }
```

### Detailed Attribute Descriptions

To understand how this data history system works, let us look at the key details stored in our database tables:

First, consider the `DOMAINS` table. This table acts as a permissions directory for the web crawler. It stores the `domain_name`, which is the unique address of each website we crawl. It tracks the `crawler_permission`, which records whether the website’s `robots.txt` file allowed us to visit. Crucially, it logs the `creator_opt_out_status`. If a creator requests that we do not use their site for training, this status is updated to block all further access.

Second, consider the `DOCUMENTS` table. This table stores the individual files we harvest. The `website_url` records the exact page address. The `license_type` tracks the legal license attached to the document. This license could be Public Domain, Creative Commons, or Proprietary. Finally, the `compliance_approval_state` records whether our review team has inspected the document and certified it as safe.

Third, consider the `PURGE_AUDITS` table. This table serves as a legal ledger of all deleted files. It records the `deleted_url`, the `deletion_reason` (such as a copyright claim or a GDPR privacy request), and the exact `date_purged` timestamp. This table provides legal proof that our database complies with international laws.

### Structured Logic for Compliance Filtering

Instead of writing complex code, database designers enforce ethics directly in the database structure. The database acts as a digital gatekeeper. It checks every scraped file before it is used for training. 

The database performs a three-step matching process to clear data:

1.  First, when the scraper harvests a new file, the system creates a document record. The database automatically links this document to its parent website.

2.  Second, the database inspects the website's crawling permissions and consent settings. It checks if the domain blocks automated crawlers or has flagged an opt-out.

3.  Third, a compliance review team inspects the harvested documents. They assign approval states to ensure that only files with safe, non-pirated license types (such as Public Domain or Creative Commons) are certified.

We can write this filter as a set of simple, logical database rules:

*   First, our **approval rule** states that a document is only eligible for training if a review team has checked it and marked it as approved.

*   Second, our **permissions constraint** immediately blocks a document if the parent website has forbidden automated web crawlers.

*   Third, our **opt-out mandate** requires that a document be immediately deleted if the original creator has requested a formal opt-out.

In practice, the system combines these rules into a single check:

*   **IF** a document is approved **AND** the website allows crawlers **AND** the creator has not requested an opt-out, **THEN** include the document in the active training dataset.

*   **IF** any of these three requirements are violated, **THEN** immediately reject the document from the dataset and log the action in our compliance ledger.

By implementing this database barrier, organizations can guarantee that if a creator decides to change their consent status, their data is immediately excluded from the next training run.

### Enforcing Purges and Erasure Logs

When a creator sends a copyright removal request or invokes their privacy rights, the data system must execute a two-step **purge process** to maintain legal and ethical integrity:

1.  The system records the document's web address, the reason for deletion (such as a GDPR erasure request), and the exact timestamp in a secure **audit log** table.

2.  The system executes a **referential purging** action. It deletes the original document from the database and flags the parent domain to ensure that future automated web crawlers can never harvest files from that website again.

By maintaining a permanent audit trail of all deleted files, the organization can prove to regulators and creators that it respects digital boundaries, building a trust-based relationship with the creative community.

---

## Discussion Questions

1.  In *The Wizard of Oz*, the Wizard speaks with a powerful voice made of many stolen voices. Does training an AI model on millions of copyrighted works count as Fair Use? Or is it copyright theft? Where should we draw the line between human learning and machine copying?

2.  Create a real-world analogy for training an AI on scraped portfolio websites. Is it like a student visiting a public museum to study painting styles? Or is it like a factory owner taking photos of local crafts to mass-produce cheap plastic replicas? Explain the strengths and weaknesses of your analogy.

3.  Our database diagram manages choice at the website level. But websites have thousands of different users. How would you change the database diagram to track choices for each individual user? What performance challenges would a database manager face when querying billions of user preferences?

4.  Under privacy laws, citizens can demand that companies delete their personal data. It is easy to delete a record from a database. But the patterns remain in the trained AI model. Does deleting a record from the database satisfy the legal right to erasure? What should a compliance team do if the AI cannot be easily un-trained?

5.  Imagine you are the database manager at a new AI startup. Write a simple three-sentence data policy for your team. Address how they must handle raw scraped data, license reviews, and sudden creator removal requests.

---

## References

*   **Books3 Dataset Takedown**: Edwards, Benj. "Books3 AI training dataset compiled of pirated books taken down." *Mashable*, 20 Aug. 2023, [mashable.com/article/books3-ai-training-dataset-pirated-books-taken-down](https://mashable.com/article/books3-ai-training-dataset-pirated-books-taken-down). This article covers the legal takedown of the Books3 dataset of pirated novels.
*   **Getty Images v. Stability AI Lawsuit**: "Getty Images Statement." *Getty Images Newsroom*, 17 Jan. 2023, [newsroom.gettyimages.com/en/getty-images/getty-images-statement](https://newsroom.gettyimages.com/en/getty-images/getty-images-statement). The official press release announcing Getty Images' legal proceedings against Stability AI for scraping millions of copyrighted stock photos.
*   **GitHub Copilot Class Action Litigation**: "GitHub Copilot Litigation." *Joseph Saveri Law Firm & Matthew Butterick*, 3 Nov. 2022, [githubcopilotlitigation.com](https://githubcopilotlitigation.com/). The official portal tracking the open-source programmers' class-action lawsuit against Microsoft, GitHub, and OpenAI.

---

## Key Takeaways

*   Every database containing scraped content must maintain an explicit record of its origin, legal license type, and date of acquisition to guarantee full data provenance.

*   User consent preferences and web crawler permissions must be stored as active constraints in the database layer so queries can automatically filter out restricted files.

*   Regular license audit queries must be executed to verify that no proprietary or unauthorized records exist in the dataset before model training begins.
